title: Colossians 1
subtitle: The Supremacy of Christ over All Creation

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — "Faith, Love, and Hope in the Gospel"

heb.paragraph: The triad of faith, love, and hope (vv. 4–5) provides the structural framework for the thanksgiving. Uniquely, ‘hope’ is identified as the ground of faith and love: ‘the faith and love that spring from the hope stored up for you in heaven.’ Hope is not a subjective feeling but an objective reality—a heavenly inheritance already secured.

refs.note: Paul’s thanksgiving for the Thessalonians’ ‘work produced by faith, labour prompted by love, and endurance inspired by hope’ provides the closest parallel to the faith-love-hope triad of Col 1:4–5.
The gospel as the ‘word of truth’ through which believers received the Spirit parallels Col 1:5–6, where the gospel is the ‘word of truth’ that produces fruit worldwide.

notes.note: Paul identifies himself as an apostle ‘by the will of God’—establishing authority over a church he did not personally found. The faith-love triad in vv. 4–5 is significant: faith is directed toward Christ, love toward fellow believers, and both spring from heavenly hope.

The gospel is ‘bearing fruit and growing throughout the whole world’ (v. 6)—a cosmic scope that anticipates the Christ-hymn’s cosmic Christology. Epaphras’s faithful ministry validates the Colossians’ initial reception of the gospel and provides a standard against which the false teaching must be measured.

Calvin notes that hope is placed as the foundation of faith and love—an unusual sequence. The explanation: heavenly hope is the objective reality on which subjective faith and practical love depend. Calvin reads the gospel’s worldwide fruitfulness (v. 6) as evidence of its divine origin: truth spreads; error eventually withers.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase tēn apokeimenēn hymin en tois ouranois (stored up for you in heaven) uses the participle of apokeimai (to be stored away, laid up), the same verb used for a reserved treasure or deposit. The NET notes the spatial metaphor: hope exists in heaven awaiting its recipients.
The phrase kathōs emathete apo Epaphra (just as you learned from Epaphras) establishes Epaphras as the authoritative teacher. The preposition apo indicates the source of the teaching.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Colossians and Philemon, Pillar (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo notes that the thanksgiving sets the stage for the letter’s argument: the gospel the Colossians received from Epaphras is complete and sufficient. The reference to the gospel’s worldwide growth (v. 6) implicitly challenges any local teaching that claims to supplement or surpass the apostolic message.

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Colossians, Philemon, WBC (1982) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien identifies the thanksgiving as programmatic: the faith-love-hope triad, grounded in the word of truth, establishes the sufficiency of the gospel the Colossians have already received. Any teaching that goes ‘beyond’ this gospel is by definition a deviation. O’Brien notes that Epaphras’s commendation (v. 7) serves a strategic purpose: by endorsing Epaphras, Paul endorses the original teaching and delegitimises the newcomers.

hist.context: Paul writes to a church he did not found, established by his associate Epaphras (v. 7). The thanksgiving (vv. 3–8) celebrates the gospel’s growth ‘all over the world’ (v. 6)—a characteristic Pauline universalism—and among the Colossians specifically since the day they ‘heard it and truly understood God’s grace’ (v. 6b). Epaphras is commended as a ‘faithful minister of Christ on our behalf’ (v. 7), lending apostolic authority to the local leadership. The placement of hope as the foundation of faith and love anticipates the letter’s eschatological emphasis: the Colossians’ attention should be directed upward (3:1–4), not toward the earthbound speculations of the false teachers.

sections.header: Verses 9–14 — "Rescued from Darkness, Transferred to the Kingdom"

heb.paragraph: The verb metestēsen (v. 13) describes a decisive act of relocation: God ‘has rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son he loves.’ The language echoes the Exodus tradition: just as God delivered Israel from Egyptian bondage, he has transferred believers from the realm of darkness to the realm of the beloved Son. The past tense indicates accomplished fact, not future hope.

refs.note: Paul’s prayer for the Ephesians—for the Spirit of wisdom, enlightened eyes, knowledge of hope, inheritance, and power—closely parallels the prayer of Col 1:9–11.
Paul’s commission to ‘open their eyes and turn them from darkness to light’ provides the missional background for the darkness-to-light transfer described in Col 1:13.

notes.note: Paul’s prayer counters the false teachers’ claim to exclusive knowledge. The ‘knowledge’ (epignōsis) Paul requests is not esoteric wisdom but practical understanding of God’s will, resulting in a worthy life. MacArthur notes the four markers of maturity: fruit-bearing, growing in knowledge, empowered endurance, and joyful thanksgiving.

The Father has accomplished three things: qualified believers for the inheritance, rescued from darkness, and transferred to the kingdom. MacArthur emphasises the past tenses: these are completed acts, not ongoing processes. Believers are already citizens of the kingdom, already redeemed, already forgiven.

Calvin reads the prayer as a corrective to intellectualism: true knowledge of God’s will issues in moral fruit, not mere speculation. The false teachers offer knowledge without transformation; Paul offers knowledge that produces worthy living.

Calvin sees in the rescue-and-transfer language the entire movement of salvation: out of Satan’s dominion, into Christ’s kingdom. The kingdom is present, not merely future: believers already live under Christ’s rule, though the kingdom’s consummation awaits.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The noun epignōsis (full knowledge, deep understanding) is a key term in Colossians (1:9, 10; 2:2; 3:10), deployed polemically against the false teachers’ claim to superior gnōsis. Paul insists that genuine knowledge comes through the Spirit, not through mystical speculation.
The noun exousia (authority, domain, dominion) applied to darkness personifies it as a ruling power. The transfer from darkness’s domain to the Son’s kingdom is a change of overlordship.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Colossians and Philemon, Pillar (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo reads the prayer as strategically designed: by asking for Spirit-given wisdom and knowledge, Paul reclaims these terms from the false teachers. The darkness-to-light transfer (vv. 13–14) provides the narrative bridge to the Christ-hymn: the Son into whose kingdom believers have been transferred is now described in cosmic terms.

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Colossians, Philemon, WBC (1982) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien identifies the prayer-report as the second part of the letter’s opening (after the thanksgiving), leading seamlessly into the Christ-hymn. The four participial clauses of vv. 10–12 (bearing fruit, growing, being strengthened, giving thanks) describe the evidence of the Spirit’s knowledge at work. The rescue language of vv. 13–14 prepares for the hymn’s cosmic reconciliation theme by establishing the personal dimension of salvation.

hist.context: Paul’s intercessory prayer (vv. 9–14) asks that the Colossians be filled with ‘the knowledge of God’s will through all the wisdom and understanding that the Spirit gives’ (v. 9)—a request that directly counters the false teachers’ claim to superior knowledge and wisdom. The prayer’s goal is practical: living ‘worthy of the Lord’ (v. 10), bearing fruit, growing in knowledge, being strengthened with power, and giving joyful thanks (vv. 10–12). The thanksgiving section (vv. 12–14) transitions into the Christ-hymn by describing the Father’s saving action: he has qualified believers to share in the inheritance of the saints in light, rescued them from darkness, and transferred them into the kingdom of his beloved Son, ‘in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins’ (v. 14).

sections.header: Verses 15–29 — "The Image of the Invisible God, Firstborn over All Creation"

heb.paragraph: The noun eikōn (v. 15) designates Christ as ‘the image of the invisible God.’ In Hellenistic philosophy, the eikōn was the visible manifestation of an invisible reality. In the OT, humanity is created in God’s image (Gen 1:26–27). Paul’s claim is that Christ is the ultimate and perfect image of God—the one in whom God’s invisible nature is fully and definitively visible.

The title prōtotokos (v. 15) denotes not temporal priority (first created) but supremacy and sovereignty. In the OT, ‘firstborn’ is a title of honour and authority (Ps 89:27; Exod 4:22). Christ is ‘firstborn over all creation’ because ‘in him all things were created’ (v. 16)—he is the agent, not the product, of creation.

The noun plērōma (v. 19) may be a term the false teachers used for the totality of divine being distributed among intermediary beings. Paul’s counterclaim is that all the fullness of God dwells in Christ alone—not dispersed among angels or elemental powers but concentrated in one person. This is developed further in 2:9.

refs.note: The Philippians Christ-hymn traces the downward trajectory (incarnation, death); the Colossian hymn emphasises the cosmic scope of Christ’s lordship over creation and the church. Together they provide complementary portraits.
The author of Hebrews describes the Son as ‘the radiance of God’s glory and the exact representation of his being’ and through whom God ‘made the universe’—closely paralleling Col 1:15–17.
The Johannine prologue’s affirmation that ‘all things were made through him’ provides the closest conceptual parallel to Col 1:16.

notes.note: Christ is not merely the highest creature but the creator of all creatures. MacArthur emphasises the fourfold ‘all things’ in v. 16: all things were created by him, through him, and for him. The thrones, powers, rulers, and authorities (v. 16) include the very spiritual beings the false teachers venerate; they were created by and are subject to Christ.

The second stanza shifts from creation to redemption. Christ is the ‘firstborn from among the dead’—sovereign over the new creation as he is over the old. The fullness dwelling in him means no supplementary spiritual being or practice is needed. Reconciliation is universal in scope (‘all things’) and achieved through the cross.

The Colossians’ personal experience of reconciliation is grounded in the cosmic reconciliation of vv. 19–20. The condition of v. 23—‘if you continue in your faith’—is not a qualification of salvation’s security but a description of its evidence: genuine faith perseveres.

Paul’s suffering ‘fills up what is still lacking in Christ’s afflictions’—not that Christ’s atoning work is insufficient, but that Christ’s body (the church) must continue to suffer in mission until the task is complete. The mystery revealed is ‘Christ in you, the hope of glory’ (v. 27)—the indwelling Christ as the guarantee of future glorification.

Calvin reads ‘image of the invisible God’ as affirming the Son’s eternal deity: the Son is not a created image but the uncreated manifestation of God’s own being. ‘Firstborn’ means not first created but supreme over all creation. Calvin uses v. 16 against every form of Arianism: the one through whom all things were created cannot himself be a creature.

Calvin identifies the ‘fullness’ as the complete divine essence: God in all his attributes dwells in Christ. The reconciliation is through blood—a real, historical sacrifice, not a mythological abstraction. Calvin notes the cosmic scope: reconciliation extends to ‘things on earth and things in heaven,’ though Calvin interprets this as the restoration of cosmic harmony disrupted by sin.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase eikōn tou theou tou aoratou (image of the invisible God) is a genitive of relationship: Christ reveals the God who is otherwise invisible. The NET notes the paradox: the invisible God becomes visible in Christ.

The four terms for cosmic powers—thronoi, kyriotētes, archai, exousiai—represent a hierarchy of spiritual beings. Whether the terms reflect a specific Jewish or proto-Gnostic angelology is debated, but Paul’s point is clear: all of them were created by and for Christ.

The phrase eirēnopoiēsas dia tou haimatos tou staurou autou (having made peace through the blood of his cross) combines cosmic reconciliation with the particularity of the cross. The NET notes the jarring juxtaposition: universal peace achieved through the scandal of crucifixion.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Colossians and Philemon, Pillar (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo reads the Christ-hymn as a direct counter to the Colossian heresy: by exalting Christ as creator, sustainer, and reconciler of all things, Paul eliminates any role for intermediary beings or supplementary practices. The plērōma language is particularly pointed: if all the fullness of God dwells in Christ, then the angel-worship, dietary regulations, and visionary experiences promoted by the false teachers are not merely unnecessary but insulting to Christ’s sufficiency. The mystery of v. 27—‘Christ in you’—is Paul’s alternative to the false teachers’ esoteric knowledge.

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Colossians, Philemon, WBC (1982) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien identifies the hymn’s two-stanza structure (creation: vv. 15–17; redemption: vv. 18–20) as providing the letter’s theological foundation. Each stanza begins with the relative pronoun hos (who, v. 15, v. 18) and builds to a culminating claim. O’Brien notes that the hymn’s cosmic scope exceeds any other Pauline christological passage: Christ is not only the agent of creation but its goal (eis auton, v. 16) and its sustainer (ta panta en autō synestēken, v. 17). The application to the Colossians (vv. 21–23) brings the cosmic theology to earth: the universal reconciler has reconciled you.

hist.context: The Christ-hymn (vv. 15–20) is the theological centre of Colossians. It is structured in two stanzas: (1) Christ and creation (vv. 15–17)—he is the image of God, firstborn over creation, creator of all things visible and invisible, prior to all things, and the one in whom all things hold together; (2) Christ and the church (vv. 18–20)—he is the head of the body, the firstborn from the dead, the one in whom all fullness dwells, and the reconciler of all things through his blood. The application (vv. 21–23) addresses the Colossians directly: they were once alienated but are now reconciled through Christ’s physical body through death. Paul’s ministry (vv. 24–29) is presented as participating in Christ’s sufferings and making known the mystery—‘Christ in you, the hope of glory’ (v. 27).

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates as the Christ-hymn affirms Christ’s absolute lordship over creation and redemption. Mission is prominent through Paul’s ministry of the mystery. Mercy and Covenant appear through the rescue-and-transfer narrative.

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Salutation: apostle by God’s will to the saints at Colossae

rows.label: vv.3–8
rows.text: Thanksgiving: faith, love, hope; the gospel bearing fruit worldwide

rows.label: vv.9–12
rows.text: Prayer: filled with knowledge, walking worthy, bearing fruit

rows.label: vv.13–14
rows.text: Rescued from darkness, transferred to the kingdom, redeemed

rows.label: vv.15–17
rows.text: Christ-hymn I: image of God, firstborn, creator, sustainer

rows.label: vv.18–20
rows.text: Christ-hymn II: head of the body, firstborn from the dead, reconciler

rows.label: vv.21–23
rows.text: Application: once alienated, now reconciled; continue in faith

rows.label: vv.24–29
rows.text: Paul’s ministry: the mystery revealed—Christ in you, the hope of glory

lit.note: Colossians 1 moves from thanksgiving (vv. 1–8) through prayer (vv. 9–14) to the Christ-hymn (vv. 15–20) and its application (vv. 21–29). The hymn’s twofold affirmation—Christ’s supremacy over creation and over the church—provides the theological foundation for the letter’s polemic against the Colossian heresy.

pairs.label: A
pairs.top: He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation (v.15)
pairs.bottom: He is the beginning, the firstborn from among the dead, so that in everything he might have the supremacy (v.18b)

pairs.label: B
pairs.top: For in him all things were created: things in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or powers or rulers or authorities (v.16a)
pairs.bottom: For God was pleased to have all his fullness dwell in him, and through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether things on earth or things in heaven (vv.19–20a)

pairs.label: C
pairs.top: All things have been created through him and for him. He is before all things, and in him all things hold together (vv.16b–17)
pairs.bottom: Making peace through his blood, shed on the cross (v.20b)

center.label: X
center.text: And he is the head of the body, the church (v.18a). Between the two stanzas — creation and reconciliation — stands the church. The one who made the cosmos now leads a community. The hymn moves from cosmic scope to intimate body, then back to cosmic scope. The church is where creation and redemption meet.

hebtext.note: The triad of faith, love, and hope (vv. 4–5) provides the structural framework for the thanksgiving. Uniquely, ‘hope’ is identified as the ground of faith and love: ‘the faith and love that spring from the hope stored up for you in heaven.’ Hope is not a subjective feeling but an objective reality—a heavenly inheritance already secured.

The verb metestēsen (v. 13) describes a decisive act of relocation: God ‘has rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son he loves.’ The language echoes the Exodus tradition: just as God delivered Israel from Egyptian bondage, he has transferred believers from the realm of darkness to the realm of the beloved Son. The past tense indicates accomplished fact, not future hope.

The noun eikōn (v. 15) designates Christ as ‘the image of the invisible God.’ In Hellenistic philosophy, the eikōn was the visible manifestation of an invisible reality. In the OT, humanity is created in God’s image (Gen 1:26–27). Paul’s claim is that Christ is the ultimate and perfect image of God—the one in whom God’s invisible nature is fully and definitively visible.

The title prōtotokos (v. 15) denotes not temporal priority (first created) but supremacy and sovereignty. In the OT, ‘firstborn’ is a title of honour and authority (Ps 89:27; Exod 4:22). Christ is ‘firstborn over all creation’ because ‘in him all things were created’ (v. 16)—he is the agent, not the product, of creation.

The noun plērōma (v. 19) may be a term the false teachers used for the totality of divine being distributed among intermediary beings. Paul’s counterclaim is that all the fullness of God dwells in Christ alone—not dispersed among angels or elemental powers but concentrated in one person. This is developed further in 2:9.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Supremacy of Christ over All Creation

translations.text: For this reason, since the day we heard about you, we have not stopped praying for you. We continually ask God to fill you with the knowledge of his will through all the wisdom and understanding that the Spirit gives,
And so, from the day we heard, we have not ceased to pray for you, asking that you may be filled with the knowledge of his will in all spiritual wisdom and understanding,
the faith and love that spring from the hope stored up for you in heaven and about which you have already heard in the true message of the gospel
because of the hope laid up for you in heaven. Of this you have heard before in the word of the truth, the gospel,

discourse.thesis: Christ is the image of the invisible God, in whom all things hold together — the head of the body, the church.

nodes.text: Paul thanks God for their faith and love, which spring from the hope stored up in heaven. The gospel is bearing fruit and growing all over the world.
Paul prays they be filled with knowledge of God's will — walking worthy, bearing fruit, being strengthened, giving thanks. The Father delivered us from darkness into the kingdom of his beloved Son.

Christ is the image of the invisible God, firstborn of all creation. All things were created through him and for him. He is before all things; in him all things hold together. He is the head of the body, the firstborn from the dead — that in everything he might be preeminent.
You who were alienated and hostile, Christ has now reconciled by his death to present you holy — if you continue in the faith, stable and steadfast.
Paul rejoices in his sufferings for their sake — filling up what is lacking in Christ's afflictions. He was made a minister to make known the mystery: Christ in you, the hope of glory.

coaching.tip: Paul writes to a church he's never visited (2:1) about a heresy he's heard secondhand. Yet his response isn't defensive correction but cosmic Christology (vv. 15-20). The heresy likely involved angelic powers and secret knowledge; Paul's answer is that Christ is supreme over all powers and is himself 'all the treasures of wisdom.' Go high when correcting low.

The Christ-hymn (vv. 15-20) moves from creation ('firstborn of all creation... all things created through him') to redemption ('firstborn from the dead... reconciling all things'). The same Christ who made the cosmos redeems it. This unity of creation and salvation combats any heresy that separates the material world from spiritual deliverance.

Paul's suffering 'fills up what is lacking in Christ's afflictions' (v. 24) — a startling phrase. Christ's atoning work is complete; what's 'lacking' is the ongoing presentation of that gospel in a hostile world, which requires suffering servants. The church extends Christ's afflictions not to add to salvation but to proclaim it.
